To add to my previous comment about `bytes` and `bytearrays`: I generally try to pay attention to mutability. Introducing `bytearray` makes the value mutable. Essentially using `bytearray` changes the data-type to something which allows dangerous operations. Using `str` instead of `bytearray` would keep the same semantics & behaviour. Unfortunately `str` has different meaning whether you look at it from Python 2 or Python 3. After giving it some thought, I would suggest using [six.binary_type](https://pythonhosted.org/six/#six.binary_type) instead of `bytearray`.

On a bytes-level getting Python2/3 compatibility can be tricky.

using `pkg_resources` adds a runtime dependency on setuptools -- which nowadays is preinstalled on most python distributions alongside pip, but technically is not part of the python standard library.
The old-school approach to get a resource filename relative to `__file__` works fine, unless you want to get a filename of a data file from a inside a zipped python package (like an .egg). But since we are including a precompiled native library, we have to set `zip=False` in setup.py in any case, otherwise we can't load it. So using pkg_resources has no concrete advantage IMO over `os.path.dirname(__file__, library_name)`

Once we agree that archiver processes should not be treated as interactive, we can start discussing how to properly solve the archiver processes' use case.

Maybe this helps. Normally the ABI encoding looks like this

FUNCID, ARG0, ARG1, ARG2
sizeof(FUNCID) := 4
sizeof(ARG?) := 32

But if one of the arguments, let say ARG1, is a dynamic argument it uses more than 32 bytes. Then it'll be something like this 
```
FUNCID, ARG0, ARG1p, ARG2, ARG0N, ARG0.0,ARG00.1,,, ARG0.N
            \______________/\
sizeof(FUNCID) := 4
sizeof(ARG?) := 32
ARG1p is the offset where the actual ARG1 is in the input. Measured in bytes from the start of the arguments block. 
ARG0N is the actual size of ARG1 meassured in number of elements

ref. http://solidity.readthedocs.io/en/develop/abi-spec.html CTRL+F "Given the contract"
```

`version` cannot be `0`. Versioning was introduced to allow working with old tokens while fixing some bugs in the library making it incompatible with the standard. This is another bug like that, meaning even version 1 tokens do not comply with the standard. When versioning was introduced, version `1` was the first one to be used, now replaced with version 2.

If a token with version `0` is found, it was definitely not defined with this library. Therefore, there's no *right* way to parse it, and it doesn't make sense bothering about it.

This is the data serialized and signed by nodes. So, the serialized data is signed, not actual one.
This data is not re-serialized on nodes side and passed to clients in serialized format. So, the client can verify the signature over serialized data.
Then it can de-serialize the data according to the serialization specified in "serialization" field.

BTW the same approach is going to be applied for Requests, where signature is calculated against serialized data, and the serialized data is passed to the pool, not actual one, and re-serializations on node side for requests is not needed.

My strong preference is to validate arguments as early as is possible; my understanding is that by setting the `choices` param when argparsing, we push the validation to when the arguments are parsed and cannot get invalid inputs here. I'd prefer to document this decision in the argparse section of the code, and to not revalidate here.

Also, this is personal preference, but I prefer to `assert` if an internal invariant fails, and to raise an exception (e.g., `IllegalArgumentException` in Java, `ValueError` in Python) if a user provides bad input. I find `assert False` to be a smell; if you're `assert False`ing, you should almost always `raise Exception("informative error message")` instead. In my world, if an `assert` fires, that indicates that there is a bug in the program that should be fixed, while raising an exception to the user tells them that they need to fix their inputs.

Hi @jwhui , the reason for not choosing Data Request with a short source address as an indicator is that Child Update Request will use extended address as source and dest, and when queueing Child Update Response message, the Data Request with a short source address may still not received(which has 100ms delay). Thus extended address will be added into the source match table, and the following Data Request with a short source address will be Acked with FramePending unset, the radio will go into sleep, and the parent will remove the child neighbor because the Child Update Response could not be Acked.

Here when `HandleChildUpdateRequest` from child, it could be sure that the child already has a short address, and following Data Request must be using short address. So `mAddSrcMatchEntryShort` is set to be true here.

```python
def _reduce(func, oids):
    while len(oids) > 1:
        oids = [_remote_reduce_helper.remote(func, item1, item2) for item1, item2 in pairwise(oids)]
    return oids[0]
```
